title: GDP per capita
subtitle: This data is adjusted for inflation and differences in living costs between countries.
caption: Across the globe in 2020, GDP per capita adjusted for purchasing power parity varies widely, with the highest values concentrated in North America, Western Europe, and parts of East Asia and Oceania. The map uses a continuous color scale ranging from light green for the lowest income levels to deep blue for the highest, with specific bands marking thresholds at $1,000, $2,000, $5,000, $10,000, and $20,000 in international dollars at 1990 prices. The United States, Canada, Australia, and Norway fall into the darkest blue band, indicating values above $20,000. Western European nations including Germany, France, the United Kingdom, and Sweden also occupy this highest tier, alongside Japan and South Korea in East Asia. In contrast, the lightest green shades, representing values below $2,000, cover large portions of sub-Saharan Africa, including countries like Niger, Chad, and the Democratic Republic of the Congo, as well as parts of South Asia such as Afghanistan and Yemen. Intermediate values, falling in the $2,000 to $10,000 range, are visible across much of Latin America—Brazil and Mexico sit in the mid-green bands—and across Eastern Europe and Russia, which appear in teal shades. China and India, while populous, are colored in lighter greens, placing them in the lower-middle income brackets relative to the global distribution. The geographic pattern shows a clear clustering of high-income economies in the northern hemisphere's temperate zones, while the lowest income levels are predominantly located in tropical and subtropical regions of Africa and Asia.
